title: Prime Factorization

projects.title: Unit 1 Project — Number System in Action
projects.desc: Apply factors, decimals, and division to a real-world capstone challenge for Unit 1.

projects.title: Factor Tree Space Salvage
projects.desc: Break numbers into their prime factors with factor trees to salvage the ship (6.NS.4).

projects.title: Factor Frenzy — Brick Breaker
projects.desc: Smash bricks by identifying factors and prime numbers in this Unit 1 arcade game.

projects.title: Prime Station: The Factor Code (Part 1)
projects.desc: Interactive graphic novel: crack the prime factorization code to reboot the station (6.NS.4).

contentObjective: I can write a number as a product of its prime factors using a factor tree.
languageObjective: I can explain how I broke a number down using the words prime number, composite number, factor, and exponent.

turnAndTalk.question: The freighter docks with 48 cargo containers, and each storage pod only holds groups built from prime-numbered units. Is 48 prime or composite, and how can you tell before building a factor tree?
turnAndTalk.kernel: 48 is composite because it has more than two factors.

stems.en: 48 is a ___ number because it has ___.
I can tell before factoring because ___.
stems.es: Puedo saberlo antes de factorizar porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students say 48 is composite because it has more than two factors (divisible by 2, 3, 4, 6, ...), so it can be broken into smaller groups.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why can a prime-numbered shipment, like 47 containers, NOT be split into smaller equal prime groups? Justify with the definition of prime.
extendStems: 47 cannot be split because its only factors are ___.
A prime number's factor tree would ___ because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Look at your factor tree for 48. How did you decide which numbers to break apart first, and how did you know when to stop?
turnAndTalk.kernel: You keep splitting until every branch is a prime number.

stems.en: I knew I was finished when every factor was ___.
stems.es: Supe que terminé cuando cada factor era ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain they keep splitting composite branches and stop when every leaf is prime, ending with 48 = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 3.
turnAndTalk.extend: Write the prime factorization of 48 using exponents and explain what the exponent means in 2⁴ × 3.
extendStems: The prime factorization of 48 with exponents is ___.

turnAndTalk.question: The cafeteria has 84 apples to arrange into equal rows. How does the prime factorization of 84 help the manager find every possible equal-row display?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Combining the prime factors of 84 builds all of its factors.

stems.en: The manager finds all the row sizes by ___ the prime factors.
stems.es: El gerente halla todos los tamaños de fila al ___ los factores primos.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students give 84 = 2 × 2 × 3 × 7 = 2² × 3 × 7 and reason that multiplying combinations of those primes produces all factors of 84, which are the possible equal-row sizes.
turnAndTalk.extend: Why does knowing the prime factorization of 84 make it faster to list all factors than checking every number from 1 to 84?
extendStems: I can build factors by ___ instead of testing every number since ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Why does every composite number have exactly one prime factorization, no matter how you start the factor tree?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Primes are unique building blocks, so the set of prime factors is always the same.

stems.en: No matter how I start the tree, I always end with ___ because ___.
stems.es: No importa cómo empiece el árbol, siempre termino con ___ porque ___.

stems.en: Prime numbers are like building blocks because ___.
stems.es: Los números primos son como bloques de construcción porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students explain that breaking down different starting splits still reduces to the same prime factors, so the prime factorization is unique (the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic in plain words).
turnAndTalk.extend: Start 48 two different ways (such as 6 × 8 and 4 × 12) and show both give 2⁴ × 3. Explain why this had to happen.
extendStems: Starting with 6 × 8 gives ___, and starting with 4 × 12 gives ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Prime Factorization, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.NS.4 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

mission.title: Cargo Codebreak
mission.story: You are the logistics officer aboard Station Helios. A shipment of 60 supply crates just docked, and the sorting robots can only distribute cargo once it is broken into its prime building blocks. Master prime factorization and the station eats this week.
mission.objective: Break each shipment quantity down into its prime factors so the robots can sort the cargo into equal pods.

scenes.name: Cargo Bay
scenes.text: Inspect the 60-crate shipment and decide how to break it into equal groups.

scenes.name: Systems Briefing
scenes.text: Learn the station's vocabulary: prime, composite, factor trees, and exponents.

scenes.name: Sorting Bay
scenes.text: Sort numbers into prime and composite bins so the robots know what to break down.

scenes.name: Robot Calibration
scenes.text: Calibrate the sorting robots. The station adapts the difficulty to keep you sharp.

scenes.name: Supply Run
scenes.text: Use prime factorization to plan every equal-row arrangement for a real supply order.

scenes.name: Mission Debrief
scenes.text: Log your findings and prove the cargo is ready for distribution.

finale.title: Cargo Cleared!
finale.text: Every crate sorted into perfect prime pods. Station Helios is fully stocked, and Mission Control just promoted you to Chief Logistics Officer — all thanks to your prime factorization mastery.

vocabulary.definition: A number bigger than 1 that you can only divide by 1 and itself.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número mayor que 1 que solo se puede dividir entre 1 y sí mismo.
vocabulary.visual: 7 has only two factors: 1 × 7. So 7 is prime.
vocabulary.cloze: A whole number greater than 1 with exactly two factors, 1 and itself, is a ___ number.

examples.why: 9 = 3 × 3, so it has more than two factors.
1 has only one factor, so it is not prime.

vocabulary.definitionVi: Số tự nhiên lớn hơn 1 chỉ có hai ước là 1 và chính nó.
vocabulary.definitionAr: عدد طبيعي أكبر من 1 له عاملان فقط: نفسه والواحد.

vocabulary.definition: A number bigger than 1 that you can divide by more than just 1 and itself.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número mayor que 1 que se puede dividir entre más números, no solo 1 y sí mismo.
vocabulary.cloze: A whole number greater than 1 that has more than two factors is a ___ number.

examples.why: 15 = 3 × 5, so it has more than two factors.
5 is prime; it has only two factors.
2 is prime; its only factors are 1 and 2.

vocabulary.definitionVi: Một số tự nhiên lớn hơn 1 có nhiều hơn hai ước.
vocabulary.definitionAr: عدد صحيح موجب أكبر من 1 له أكثر من عاملين.

vocabulary.definition: Writing a number as prime numbers multiplied together.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Escribir un número como números primos multiplicados.
vocabulary.termVi: Phân tích nhân tử nguyên tố
vocabulary.definitionVi: Viết một số dưới dạng tích của các số nguyên tố.
vocabulary.definitionAr: كتابة عدد صحيح على شكل حاصل ضرب أعداد أولية فقط.

vocabulary.definition: A picture that splits a number into its prime numbers, step by step.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un dibujo que separa un número en sus números primos, paso a paso.
vocabulary.cloze: I can break a number into its prime factors step by step using a ___.
vocabulary.definitionVi: Sơ đồ hình cây dùng để tìm các thừa số nguyên tố của một số.
vocabulary.definitionAr: مخطط هيكلي يستخدم لإيجاد العوامل الأولية للعدد.

vocabulary.definition: A small number that tells how many times to multiply a number by itself.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un número pequeño que dice cuántas veces multiplicar un número por sí mismo.
vocabulary.cloze: In 2³, the small number 3 is the ___ and it shows 2 is multiplied 3 times.

examples.why: It tells how many times 2 is multiplied by itself.
The 2 is the base, not the exponent.
Here 5 is a factor, not an exponent.

vocabulary.definitionVi: Số cho biết số cơ số được nhân với chính nó bao nhiêu lần.
vocabulary.definitionAr: الرقم الذي يوضح عدد مرات ضرب الرقم الأساسي في نفسه.

launch.narrative: The space station received 60 supply crates. Mission Control needs to break this quantity into its prime components so the sorting robots can distribute them into equally sized pods. Help the crew find the prime factorization of 60!
launch.contextImage: Space station cargo bay with robots sorting crates into smaller equal groups
noticePrompts: What number are we breaking down into factors?
What's the difference between a factor and a prime factor?
wonderPrompts: How many different ways can we start breaking 60 apart?
Will we always get the same prime factors no matter how we start?

conceptIntro.heading: What is prime factorization?
conceptIntro.intro: Prime factorization means writing a number as a product of prime numbers only. A prime number can be divided only by 1 and itself, like 2, 3, 5, and 7.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: I want the prime factorization of 60. I start by splitting it: 60 = 6 × 10.
6 is not prime, so I break it: 6 = 2 × 3. Both 2 and 3 are prime, so I stop those branches.
10 is not prime, so I break it: 10 = 2 × 5. Both 2 and 5 are prime, so I stop.
Using exponents, I write it as 2² × 3 × 5. I check: 2 × 2 × 3 × 5 = 60. Correct.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Let's factor 12 together. What is one way to split 12 into two factors? (You might say 3 × 4.)
Is 3 prime? Yes, so we keep it. Is 4 prime? No — what is 4 split apart? (4 = 2 × 2.)
So put the prime factors together: 12 = 2 × 2 × 3. Every factor is prime, so we are done.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next, you will sort numbers into prime and composite, then build factor trees on your own.
Remember: keep splitting until every branch is a prime number.

explore.instructions: Sort these numbers — which are prime and which are composite?

discourse.prompt: How did you decide whether a number is prime or composite? What strategy did you use?
discourse.sentenceFrame: I know ___ is prime because its only factors are ___ and ___. I know ___ is composite because it has factors like ___.

approaching.stem: Which of the following is a prime number?
approaching.explanation: 17 has exactly two factors: 1 and 17. 15 = 3 × 5, 21 = 3 × 7, and 9 = 3 × 3, so they are all composite.

approaching.stem: What is the prime factorization of 30?
approaching.explanation: 30 = 2 × 15 = 2 × 3 × 5. All three factors (2, 3, 5) are prime, so 2 × 3 × 5 is the prime factorization.

approaching.stem: What is the prime factorization of 18?
approaching.explanation: 18 = 2 × 9 = 2 × 3 × 3. Both 2 and 3 are prime, so 2 × 3 × 3 is the prime factorization.

approaching.instructions: Sort each expression: is it a COMPLETE prime factorization, or does it still have composite factors?

approaching.stem: Which of these numbers is composite?
approaching.explanation: 27 = 3 × 9 = 3 × 3 × 3, so it has more than two factors. 23, 29, and 31 are all prime.

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Prime Factorization.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.

onLevel.label: Match each number to its prime factorization.
Build the factor tree. Write each number as a product of primes.

onLevel.instructions: Sort each number: can it be expressed as a product of exactly TWO prime factors, or does it need THREE or more?

categories.label: Product of Three or More Primes

onLevel.stem: Two students found different factor trees for 60. Student A started with 2 × 30. Student B started with 6 × 10. Which statement is true?
choices: Both get the same prime factorization: 2 × 2 × 3 × 5
Only Student A gets the correct prime factorization
Only Student B gets the correct prime factorization
They will get different prime factorizations
onLevel.explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic says every composite number has exactly one prime factorization. No matter how you start the factor tree, you always end with 2 × 2 × 3 × 5.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Prime Factorization.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.

extending.title: Find Marcus's Mistake
extending.correctWork: 8 = 2 × 4, but 4 is not prime. 4 = 2 × 2, so 8 = 2 × 2 × 2. The correct prime factorization is 48 = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 3 = 2⁴ × 3.
hints: Look at Step 4 — is every factor in the answer a prime number?
4 is not prime because 4 = 2 × 2. Marcus needed to keep breaking it down.

extending.prompt: Choose any two-digit composite number. Show TWO different factor trees that both lead to the same prime factorization. Explain why every composite number has only one prime factorization.
extending.sentenceFrame: I chose the number ___. My first factor tree starts with ___ × ___, and my second starts with ___ × ___. Both give the same prime factorization: ___. This happens because ___.

extending.label: Write the prime factorization using exponents.

optionalActivity.name: Prime Factorization Escape Lab
optionalActivity.intro: The station's prime-code vault is locked. Crack each lock using factor trees, exponents, and prime clues to escape.

optional.stem: Lock 1 — Access clue: A whole number greater than 1 with exactly two factors (1 and itself) is called what?
optional.explanation: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself. A composite number has more than two factors.

optional.stem: Lock 2 — Build the factor tree for 72. What is its prime factorization?
Lock 3 — Rewrite the prime factorization of 72 in exponent form.

optional.instructions: Lock 4 — Scan each access code. Is it a COMPLETE prime factorization, or does it still contain a composite factor?

optional.stem: Final Vault — The vault code is the prime factorization of 84 with all prime factors listed. Which code opens it?

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Repair the Broken Factor Tree
optional.correctWork: 8 = 2 × 4, but 4 is not prime. 4 = 2 × 2, so 8 = 2 × 2 × 2. The correct prime factorization is 56 = 2 × 2 × 2 × 7 = 2³ × 7.
hints: Look at the final answer — is every factor a prime number?
4 is not prime because 4 = 2 × 2. Keep breaking it down until only primes remain.

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Prime Factorization is skipping the key idea: "Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: A party planner has 72 balloons and wants to know every possible way to arrange them in equal rows. She uses prime factorization to find all the factors of 72.
connect.promptQuestion: How does prime factorization help the planner find all the ways to arrange the balloons?
connect.prompt: The prime factorization of 72 is ___, which helps me find all factors by ___.

exitTicket.stem: What is the prime factorization of 40?

learningTonight.en: Break each shipment quantity down into its prime factors so the robots can sort the cargo into equal pods.

conceptSteps.en: You are the logistics officer aboard Station Helios.
conceptSteps.es: Lean la historia de la misión juntos antes de empezar los ejercicios.

conceptSteps.en: Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.
conceptSteps.es: Verifiquen cada paso con la idea clave de la lección.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Prime number" — A number bigger than 1 that you can only divide by 1 and itself.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Número primo" — Un número mayor que 1 que solo se puede dividir entre 1 y sí mismo.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Composite number" — A number bigger than 1 that you can divide by more than just 1 and itself.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Número compuesto" — Un número mayor que 1 que se puede dividir entre más números, no solo 1 y sí mismo.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Prime factorization" — Writing a number as prime numbers multiplied together.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Factorización prima" — Escribir un número como números primos multiplicados.

tryTogether.scenarioEn: You are the logistics officer aboard Station Helios. A shipment of 60 supply crates just docked, and the sorting robots can only distribute cargo once it is broken into its prime building blocks. Master prime factorization and the station eats this week.
tryTogether.scenarioEs: Usen la historia de la misión para conectar la matemática con un problema real.

steps.en: Pick one practice problem and solve it on paper together.
steps.es: Elijan un problema de práctica y resuélvanlo en papel juntos.
steps.hintEs: Hablen de cada paso en voz alta.

steps.en: Check: does your answer follow — Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.?
steps.es: Verifiquen: ¿su respuesta sigue — Keep breaking a number apart until every factor is a prime number you cannot split anymore.?
steps.hint: This is the flagship lesson's deepest idea.
steps.hintEs: Esta es la idea más profunda de la lección estrella.

say.en: What part of the mission story helps here?
say.es: ¿Qué parte de la historia de la misión ayuda aquí?

say.en: Let's re-read the key idea together.

dontSay.en: This flagship lesson is too hard — skip it.
dontSay.es: Esta lección estrella es muy difícil — sáltenla.

printables.name: Prime Factorization Escape Folder
printables.desc: Teams crack a chain of prime-factorization clues, each revealing a code digit, to open a final lock.

printables.name: Prime Factorization — Color by Number
printables.desc: Solve each problem, then color by the answer to reveal a picture.
